2 Data and Analysis
All data and analyses are provided for research and educational purposes.

title: Home
text:
Welcome to the kincounts research website.


This project estimates the probability of identifying individuals through genetic databases by modeling extended family relationships. It simulates the distribution of distant relatives (cousins) based on fertility patterns and calculates the likelihood of finding genetic matches in a database.




Fertility Distribution Estimation: Fit zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) models to fertility count data
Relative Count Simulation: Generate simulated counts of relatives at various degrees
Identity Probability Calculation: Estimate the probability of finding genetic matches in databases




We provide a Shiny web application for interactive exploration of the models. The app includes:

Parameter estimation from uploaded fertility data
Customizable simulations with adjustable parameters
Visualization of relative count distributions
Identity probability calculations based on population and database sizes




The application uses a zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) model to capture fertility patterns, including childlessness. Genetic match probabilities are calculated based on the expected length and number of shared DNA segments between cousins of varying degrees.




analysis/: R Markdown analysis files
app/: Shiny application code
code/: Utility functions and analysis scripts
data/: Example datasets
docs/: Generated website files
output/: Analysis outputs




Explore the analyses using the navigation bar above, or run the Shiny application locally:
shiny::runApp("app/app.R")

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
We extract the fertility information for U.S. women from the Integrated Public Use Microdata Series (IPUMS) dataset.




Code
path         <- if (dir.exists("data")) "data" else file.path("..", "data")
output_path  <- if (dir.exists("output")) "output" else file.path("..", "output")
file_path    <- file.path(path, "mother_data.csv")

## original script loads both; we only need mother_data for this step
mother_data <- read_csv(file_path, show_col_types = FALSE)


mother_data already contains:

Year – Census year (1960,1970,1980,1990)
Age – respondent age
Race – self-identified race of the respondent.
n_child – the number of children ever born to each woman




We focus on U.S. women aged 50-59, who are considered to have completed their fertility. The Census data include observations from 1960, 1970, 1980, and 1990. Women who were aged 50-59 in 1950 are observed as ages 60-69 in the 1960 Census. We therefore prepare fertility data for U.S. women aged 50-59 in 1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, and 1990, corresponding to the 1891-1900, 1901-1910, 1911-1920, 1921-1930, and 1931-1940 birth cohorts, respectively.


Code
fertility_data <- bind_rows(
  mother_data %>%
    filter(Year %in% c(1960, 1970, 1980, 1990),
           Age >= 50, Age <= 59),
  mother_data %>%
    filter(Year == 1960,
           Age >= 60, Age <= 69) %>%
    transmute(
      Year = 1950,
      Age = Age - 10,
      Race = Race,
      n_child = n_child
    )
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(n_child)) %>%
  arrange(Year, Age)

fertility_data %>%
  count(Year, name = "n_women") %>%
  kable(caption = "Prepared fertility data by census year and age.")

Code
plot_census_pmf <- function(fertility_df, sibling_df, max_count = 12) {
  pmf_df <- bind_rows(
    fertility_df %>%
      transmute(
        Year,
        Distribution = "Fertility",
        count = pmin(n_child, max_count)
      ),
    sibling_df %>%
      transmute(
        Year,
        Distribution = "Sibling",
        count = pmin(n_sib, max_count)
      )
  ) %>%
    count(Year, Distribution, count, name = "n_counts") %>%
    group_by(Year, Distribution) %>%
    mutate(prob = n_counts / sum(n_counts)) %>%
    ungroup()
  
  count_levels <- 0:max_count
  count_labels <- as.character(count_levels)
  count_labels[length(count_labels)] <- paste0(max_count, "+")
  
  pmf_df %>%
    mutate(
      signed_prob = if_else(Distribution == "Fertility", -prob, prob),
      count_cat = factor(count, levels = count_levels, labels = count_labels),
      Year = factor(Year)
    ) %>%
    ggplot(aes(x = count_cat, y = signed_prob, fill = count_cat, alpha = Distribution)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.82) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", linewidth = 0.4) +
    coord_flip() +
    facet_wrap(~ Year, ncol = 3) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) abs(x)) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = viridis(length(count_levels), option = "D"), name = "Count") +
    scale_alpha_manual(values = c("Fertility" = 0.7, "Sibling" = 1), guide = "none") +
    labs(
      #title = "Probability mass functions (PMF) of fertility and sibling distributions across years",
      x = "Count",
      y = "Probability",
      # caption = "Left: Fertility distribution; Right: Sibling distribution"
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
      axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
      plot.caption = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank()
    )
}

plot_census_pmf(fertility_data, sib_data)









Code
ggsave(file = file.path(output_path, "census_fertility_sibling_pmf.pdf"), width = 11, height = 7)




Code
mean_across_years <- bind_rows(
  fertility_data %>%
    group_by(Year) %>%
    summarise(
      Distribution = "Fertility",
      Mean = mean(n_child),
      .groups = "drop"
    ),
  sib_data %>%
    group_by(Year) %>%
    summarise(
      Distribution = "Sibling",
      Mean = mean(n_sib),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
)

mean_across_years %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, y = Mean, color = Distribution, group = Distribution)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = sort(unique(mean_across_years$Year))) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Fertility" = "#0072B2", "Sibling" = "#D55E00"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    # title = "Mean Fertility and Sibling Counts Across Census Years",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Mean family size"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )









Code
ggsave(file = file.path(output_path, "mean_fertility_sibling_across_years.pdf"), width = 8, height = 5)

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
2.1 Fertility distribution
We model the number of children per family using a Poisson distribution and a zero inflated negative binomial distribution (ZINB), respectively.

2.1.1 Poisson distribution
The Poisson distribution is a common model for count data, particularly when the mean and variance are equal. However, it may not be suitable for fertility data, which often exhibit zero-inflation and overdispersion (variance greater than the mean). The Poisson distribution with mean fertility (\(\mu\)) is defined as: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[P(num_{child}=k)=e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^k}{k!},\] denoted by \(Poisson(\mu)\). The mean and variance of \(Poisson(\mu)\) are both \(\mu\).


2.1.2 Zero inflated negative binomial distribution
The zero inflated negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\mu\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta\)), and zero‑inflation probability (\(\pi_0\))—denoted as \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\)—is defined as: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[P(num_{child}=k)=\begin{cases}
\pi_0 + (1-\pi_0)p^{\theta} & \text{if } k=0, \\
(1-\pi_0)\binom{k+\theta-1}{k}(1-p)^k p ^{\theta} & \text{if } k \geq 1,
\end{cases}\] where \(p = \frac{\theta}{\mu+\theta}\) represents the success probability of the negative binomial distribution.
According to the definition of the negative binomial distribution, the mean and variance of \(NB(\mu,\theta)\) are given by \(\mu\) and \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\), respectively. Therefore, the mean and variance of \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\) are defined by \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) and \(\mu(1-\pi_0) + \frac{\mu^2(1-\pi_0)}{\theta}+\mu^2\pi_0(1-\pi_0)\), respectively.

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
2.2 Sibling distribution
The sibling distribution can be derived from the fertility distribution. For any count \(k \geq 0\), the probability that an individual has \(k\) siblings is proportional to the probability that their mother has \(k+1\) children, multiplied by the number of ways the individual could be one of those children. Therefore, the sibling distribution satisfies: \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(num_{child}=k+1).\]

2.2.1 Derivation of sibling distribution
If the fertility distribution is \(Poisson(\mu)\), then the sibling distribution is given by: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^{k+1}}{(k+1)!} = e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^{k+1}}{k!}\propto e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^k}{k!},\] which is also a Poisson distribution with mean \(\mu\).
If the fertility distribution is \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\), then the sibling distribution is given by: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[\begin{align*}
P(num_{sib}=k) & \propto (1-\pi_0)(k+1)\binom{k+\theta}{k+1}(1-p)^{k+1} p^{\theta} \\
& = (1-\pi_0)(k+1)\frac{(k+\theta)!}{(k+1)!(\theta-1)!}(1-p)^{k+1} p^{\theta} \\
& \propto \frac{\theta(1-p)}{p} \frac{(k+\theta)!}{k!\theta!}(1-p)^k p^{\theta+1} \\
& \propto \binom{k+\theta}{k}(1-p)^k p^{\theta+1}, \\
\end{align*}\] where \(p = \frac{\theta}{\mu+\theta}\). Therefore, the sibling distribution is a negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta+1\)), denoted as \(NB(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}, \theta+1)\). The variance is \(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta} + \frac{\mu^2(\theta+1)}{\theta^2}\).

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
section: 3.1 Fit the Poisson and ZINB models
text:
3.1 Fit the Poisson and ZINB models


Code
fit_fert <- function(dat) {
  ## Poisson model
  pois <- glm(n_child ~ 1, family = poisson, data = dat)
  
  ## Zero‑inflated negative binomial model
  zinb <- zeroinfl(n_child ~ 1 | 1, data = dat, dist = "negbin")
  
  tibble(
    Model = c("Poisson", "ZINB"),
    mu    = c(exp(coef(pois)[1]),                    ## log‑link → exp() gives mean
              exp(zinb$coefficients$count[1])),
    size  = c(Inf,                                   ## dispersion θ; Inf = Poisson
              zinb$theta),
    pi0   = c(0,                                      ## zero‑inflation prob.
              plogis(zinb$coefficients$zero[1]))
  )
}

param_fert <- fertility_data %>%
  group_by(Year) %>%
  group_modify(~ fit_fert(.x)) %>%
  ungroup()

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
section: 3.3 Compare the mean and variance from the models and data
text:
3.3 Compare the mean and variance from the models and data


Code
cal_mean_var <- function(model, mu, size, pi0) {
  if (model == "Poisson") {
    mean_fert = mu
    var_fert  = mu
    mean_sib = mu
    var_sib  = mu
  } else if (model == "ZINB") {
    mean_fert = mu * (1 - pi0)
    var_fert  = mu * (1 - pi0) + (mu^2 * (1 - pi0) / size) + (mu^2 * pi0 * (1 - pi0))
    mean_sib = mu * ((size + 1) / size)
    var_sib  = mu * ((size + 1) / size) + (mu^2 * ((size + 1) / size) / size)
  }
  
  tibble(
    Model = model,
    Mean_Fertility = mean_fert,
    Variance_Fertility = var_fert,
    Mean_Sibling = mean_sib,
    Variance_Sibling = var_sib
  )
}

mean_var_fitted <- param_fert %>%
  rowwise(Year, Model) %>%
  reframe(cal_mean_var(Model, mu, size, pi0)) %>%
  ungroup()

mean_var_data <- bind_rows(
  fertility_data %>%
    group_by(Year) %>%
    summarise(
      distribution = "fertility",
      mean_data = mean(n_child),
      variance_data = var(n_child),
      .groups = "drop"
    ),
  sib_data %>%
    group_by(Year) %>%
    summarise(
      distribution = "sibling",
      mean_data = mean(n_sib),
      variance_data = var(n_sib),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
)

mean_var_model <- mean_var_fitted %>%
  transmute(
    Year,
    Model = tolower(Model),
    mean_fertility = Mean_Fertility,
    variance_fertility = Variance_Fertility,
    mean_sibling = Mean_Sibling,
    variance_sibling = Variance_Sibling
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_fertility, variance_fertility, mean_sibling, variance_sibling),
    names_to = c(".value", "distribution"),
    names_pattern = "(mean|variance)_(fertility|sibling)"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Model,
    values_from = c(mean, variance),
    names_glue = "{.value}_{Model}"
  )

mean_var <- mean_var_data %>%
  left_join(mean_var_model, by = c("distribution", "Year")) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    distribution,
    Year,
    mean_data,
    mean_poisson,
    mean_zinb,
    variance_data,
    variance_poisson,
    variance_zinb
  ) %>%
  arrange(distribution, Year)

write.csv(mean_var, file.path(output_path, "fertility_estimation.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

kable(mean_var, digits = 3, caption = "Mean and variance of fertility and sibling distributions from the fitted models and data.")

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
3.4 Model evaluation
Based on the table of mean and variance, we can see that the fertility data exhibits overdispersion (variance greater than mean). The Poisson Model assumes equal mean and variance, which does not fit the data well. The ZINB Model accounts for overdispersion and zero-inflation, providing a better fit to the data. The ZINB model’s mean and variance estimates for both fertility and sibling distributions align more closely with the observed data.

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
section: 4.1 Simulate the fertility and sibling counts

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
section: 4.2 Compare sibling counts from simulations with theoretical values
text:
4.2 Compare sibling counts from simulations with theoretical values
We run simulations for fertility means of 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, and 4.0 for both Poisson and ZINB models. Additionally, in ZINB models, we set the dispersion parameter \(\theta\) to 4, and the zero-inflation probability \(\pi_0\) to 0.05.
We compare the empirical mean and variance of sibling counts from the simulated data with the theoretical values. The comparison shows that the theoretical values align closely with the simulated results, confirming the correctness of our derivation.


Code
means = c(1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0)
size = 4
pi0 = 0.05

cal_mean_var_simulated <- function(sim_data) {
  sib_counts <- sim_data$sib_counts
  
  mean_sib = mean(sib_counts)
  var_sib = var(sib_counts)
  
  tibble(
    Mean_Sibling = mean_sib,
    Variance_Sibling = var_sib
  )
}

# Poisson Model
simulated_poisson <- lapply(means, function(mu) {
  simulate_fert_sib("Poisson", mu, Inf, 0)
})

simulated_poisson_results <- lapply(simulated_poisson, cal_mean_var_simulated) %>% bind_rows() %>%
  mutate(Fertility_Parameter = sapply(means, function(mu) paste0("Fertility Mean = ", mu)),
         Value_Type = "Simulated Poisson") %>% dplyr::select(Fertility_Parameter, Value_Type, everything())
threo_possion_results <- tibble(
  Fertility_Parameter = sapply(means, function(mu) paste0("Fertility Mean = ", mu)),
  Value_Type = "Theoretical Poisson",
  Mean_Sibling = means,
  Variance_Sibling = means
)

poisson_results <- bind_rows(simulated_poisson_results, threo_possion_results) %>%
  arrange(Fertility_Parameter) %>%
  mutate(Value_Type = factor(Value_Type, levels = c("Theoretical Poisson", "Simulated Poisson")))

kable(poisson_results, digits = 3, caption = "Mean and Variance of Sibling counts from simulations and theoretical values based on Poisson distribution.")

         Value_Type = "Simulated ZINB") %>% dplyr::select(Fertility_Parameter, Value_Type, everything())

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
4.3 Visualize sibling distributions
We generate discrete probability distributions for sibling counts based on both Poisson and ZINB models, with the y-axis representing the number of siblings and the x-axis representing the probability of having that many siblings. The fertility mean is set to 2.5, and the ZINB model has a dispersion parameter of 4 and a zero-inflation probability of 0.05.


Code
library(viridisLite)

plot_pmf <- function(pmf_df) {
  cats = sort(unique(pmf_df$sib_counts))
  n_cat <- length(cats)
  color_palette <- viridis(n_cat, option = "D")
  df <- pmf_df %>%
    mutate(y = ifelse(Model == "Poisson", -prob, prob),
           count_cat = factor(sib_counts, levels = cats))
  ggplot(df, aes(x = count_cat, y = y, fill = count_cat, alpha = Model)) +
    geom_col() +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", size = 0.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) abs(x)) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = color_palette, name = "Siblings Count") +
    scale_alpha_manual(values = c("Poisson" = 0.7, "ZINB" = 1), guide = "none") +
    labs(
      title = "PMF of Siblings Counts (Simulated from Poisson and ZINB Models)",
      x = "Number of Siblings",
      y = "Probability",
      caption = "Poisson (left, negative y), ZINB (right, positive y)."
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
      axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
      plot.caption = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
      legend.position = "right",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank()
    )
}




Code
# fertility parameters
mean = 2.5
size = 4
pi0 = 0.05
max_sibs = 12

# simulated data for fertility mean 2.5
simulated_data <- bind_rows(
  tibble(sib_counts = simulate_fert_sib("Poisson", mean, Inf, 0)$sib_counts) %>%
    mutate(Model = "Poisson"),
  tibble(sib_counts = simulate_fert_sib("ZINB", mean/(1-pi0), size, pi0)$sib_counts) %>%
    mutate(Model = "ZINB")
)

simulated_data$sib_counts <- ifelse(simulated_data$sib_counts > max_sibs, max_sibs, simulated_data$sib_counts)

pmf_data <- simulated_data %>%
  count(Model, sib_counts, name = "n_counts") %>%
  group_by(Model) %>%
  mutate(prob = n_counts / sum(n_counts)) %>%
  ungroup()

# write the pmf data to a csv file
write.csv(pmf_data, file = file.path(output_path, "pmf_sibling_counts.csv"), row.names = FALSE)




Code
plot_pmf(pmf_data)

ggsave(file = file.path(output_path, "pmf_sibling_counts.png"), width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 500)

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
text:
Here we establish a zero inflated negative binomial distribution for the number of children per family—fertility distribution with parameters: mean fertility (\(\mu\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta\)), and zero‑inflation probability (\(\pi_0\)), denoted as \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\). According to the definition of the negative binomial distribution, the mean and variance of \(NB(\mu,\theta)\) is given by \(\mu\) and \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\), respectively. Therefore, the mean and variance of \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\) is defined by \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) and \(\mu(1-\pi_0) + \frac{\mu^2(1-\pi_0)}{\theta}+\mu^2\pi_0(1-\pi_0)\), respectively.



The sibling distribution is the distribution of the number of siblings per person, which can be derived from the fertility distribution. For any \(k \geq 0\), the probability of having \(k\) siblings is given by: \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(num_{child}=k+1).\] This is because a person with \(k\) siblings must have been born into a family with \(k+1\) children, and there are \(k+1\) ways to choose which child they are among those children. If the fertility distribution is \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\), then the sibling distribution is a negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta+1\)), denoted as \(NB(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}, \theta+1)\). We note that the average number of siblings per person \(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}\) is higher than the average number of children \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) minus one, called the “sibship size paradox”.



Based on the empirical data on U.S. women, a reasonable range of parameters for the fertility distribution is \[\mu \in [2, 6], ~ \theta \in [1, 10], ~ and ~ \pi_0 \in [0, 0.2].\]

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
text: Here we establish a zero inflated negative binomial distribution for the number of children per family—fertility distribution with parameters: mean fertility (\(\mu\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta\)), and zero‑inflation probability (\(\pi_0\)), denoted as \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\). According to the definition of the negative binomial distribution, the mean and variance of \(NB(\mu,\theta)\) is given by \(\mu\) and \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\), respectively. Therefore, the mean and variance of \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\) is defined by \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) and \(\mu(1-\pi_0) + \frac{\mu^2(1-\pi_0)}{\theta}+\mu^2\pi_0(1-\pi_0)\), respectively.

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
text: The sibling distribution is the distribution of the number of siblings per person, which can be derived from the fertility distribution. For any \(k \geq 0\), the probability of having \(k\) siblings is given by: \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(num_{child}=k+1).\] This is because a person with \(k\) siblings must have been born into a family with \(k+1\) children, and there are \(k+1\) ways to choose which child they are among those children. If the fertility distribution is \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\), then the sibling distribution is a negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta+1\)), denoted as \(NB(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}, \theta+1)\). We note that the average number of siblings per person \(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}\) is higher than the average number of children \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) minus one, called the “sibship size paradox”.

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
text: Based on the empirical data on U.S. women, a reasonable range of parameters for the fertility distribution is \[\mu \in [2, 6], ~ \theta \in [1, 10], ~ and ~ \pi_0 \in [0, 0.2].\]

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
section: 2.1 Simulate the relative counts for the focal individual

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
text:
2.2 Prepare parameter combinations


Code
# Prepare fertility parameter tables
fert_params = expand.grid(
  mu = c(3, 4, 5, 6),
  size = c(1, 4, 10),
  pi0 = c(0, 0.1, 0.2)
)
fert_params = as.data.frame(fert_params)[, c("pi0", "size", "mu")]
n_sim = 10000  # Number of simulations per parameter combination

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
section: 2.3 Simulate relative counts for each parameter combination
text:
2.3 Simulate relative counts for each parameter combination


Code
relative_counts = list()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(fert_params))) {
  params = fert_params[i, ]
  relative_counts[[i]] = simulate_relatives_zinb(
    mu = params$mu,
    size = params$size,
    pi0 = params$pi0,
    n_sim = n_sim
  )
}

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
text:
2.5 Visualize the simulated relative distributions


Code
library(ggplot2)
# Combine all relative counts into a single data frame
relative_counts_df = bind_rows(relative_counts)
relative_counts_df = relative_counts_df %>%
  mutate(
    mu = rep(fert_params$mu, each = n_sim),
    size = rep(fert_params$size, each = n_sim),
    pi0 = rep(fert_params$pi0, each = n_sim)
  )
# Pivot to long format for plotting
relative_counts_long = relative_counts_df %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Siblings, AuntsUncles, Cousins), 
               names_to = "RelativeType", values_to = "Count") %>% 
  mutate(
    mu = factor(mu, labels = c("3", "4", "5", "6")),
    RelativeType = factor(RelativeType, levels = c("Siblings", "AuntsUncles", "Cousins")),
    size = factor(size, labels = c("theta = 1", "theta = 4", "theta = 10")),
    pi0 = factor(pi0, labels = c("pi_0 = 0", "pi_0 = 0.1", "pi_0 = 0.2"))
  )

# Plot the distributions
ggplot(relative_counts_long,
       aes(x = mu, y = Count, fill = RelativeType)) +
  geom_boxplot(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
               width = 0.7, alpha = .8, outlier.size = .3) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 21, size = 1.4,
               aes(group = RelativeType),
               position = position_dodge(width = 0.8),
               colour = "black", fill = "white") +
  facet_grid(pi0 ~ size) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "D") +
  labs(title   = "Simulated relative distributions under different fertility parameters",
       x       = "Mean fertility (mu)",
       y       = "Relative count",
       fill    = "Relative type") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")





Simulated relative distributions under different fertility parameters




Based on the simulated relative distributions, we observe the following patterns: the number of relatives increases with higher mean fertility (\(\mu\)), and decreases as the dispersion parameter (\(\theta\)) increases—–since greater dispersion corresponds to lower fertility variance. Similarly, the number of relatives declines as the zero-inflation probability (\(\pi_0\)) increases, though this effect is less pronounced. In general, the number of siblings is lower than that of aunts and uncles, which in turn is lower than the number of cousins—–consistent with the real family structure.

title: How fertility parameters affect the family structure distribution
section: 2.6 Regression Model to analyze the effects of fertility parameters
text:
2.6 Regression Model to analyze the effects of fertility parameters

Code
# Prepare wide fertility parameter tables
fert_params = expand.grid(
  mu = c(2, 3, 4, 5, 6),
  size = c(1, 2, 4, 6, 10),
  pi0 = c(0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2)
)
fert_params = as.data.frame(fert_params)[, c("pi0", "size", "mu")]
n_sim = 10000  # Number of simulations per parameter combination

# Simulate relative counts for each parameter combination
relative_counts = list()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(fert_params))) {
  params = fert_params[i, ]
  relative_counts[[i]] = simulate_relatives_zinb(
    mu = params$mu,
    size = params$size,
    pi0 = params$pi0,
    n_sim = n_sim
  )
}

# Combine all relative counts into a single data frame
relative_counts_df = bind_rows(relative_counts)
relative_counts_df = relative_counts_df %>%
  mutate(
    mu = rep(fert_params$mu, each = n_sim),
    size = rep(fert_params$size, each = n_sim),
    pi0 = rep(fert_params$pi0, each = n_sim)
  )

library(broom)

relatives <- c("Siblings", "AuntsUncles", "Cousins")

for (rel in relatives) {
  cat("\n\n### Focal-individual model for", rel, "\n\n")
  form <- as.formula(paste0(rel, " ~ mu + size + pi0"))
  lm_fit <- lm(form, data = relative_counts_df)
  lm_tidy <- broom::tidy(lm_fit, conf.int = TRUE)
  print(kable(
    lm_tidy,
    digits = 3,
    caption = paste(
      "Regression coefficients for", rel, 
      "(focal-individual)"
    )
  ))
}

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text: In this article, we develop a statistical framework for modelling the relationship between the fertility distribution and sibling distribution: Given a fertility distribution, we derive what the resulting sibling distribution would be in the next generation. Specifically, the fertility distribution describes the number of children per woman, while the sibling distribution describes the number of siblings per person in the next generation.

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text:
1.1 Poisson distribution
The Poisson distribution is a popular model for count data, particularly when the mean and variance are equal. However, it may not be very suitable for fertility data, which often exhibit zero-inflation and overdispersion (variance greater than the mean). The Poisson distribution with mean fertility (\(\mu\)) is defined as: for the number of children \(k \geq 0\), \[P(N_{child}=k)=e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^k}{k!}, \tag{1}\] denoted by \(\text{Poisson}(\mu)\). The mean and variance of \(\text{Poisson}(\mu)\) are both \(\mu\).

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text:
1.2 Zero inflated negative binomial distribution
The zero inflated negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\mu\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta\)), and zero‑inflation probability (\(\pi_0\))—denoted as \(\text{ZINB}(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\)—is defined as: for the number of children \(k \geq 0\), \[P(N_{child}=k)=\begin{cases}
\pi_0 + (1-\pi_0)p^{\theta} & \text{if } k=0, \\
(1-\pi_0)\binom{k+\theta-1}{k}(1-p)^k p ^{\theta} & \text{if } k \geq 1,
\end{cases} \tag{2}\] where \(p = \frac{\theta}{\mu+\theta}\) is denoted as the success probability. For the negative binomial distribution \(\text{NB}(\mu,\theta)\), the mean and variance are \(\mu\) and \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\). Therefore, the mean and variance of \(\text{ZINB}(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\) are given by \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) and \(\mu(1-\pi_0) + \frac{\mu^2(1-\pi_0)}{\theta}+\mu^2\pi_0(1-\pi_0)\), respectively.

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text:
2.1 Sibship size paradox
A common mistake is that the sibling distribution should be the same as the fertility distribution minus one. However, this is not the case due to the sibship size paradox: individuals with more siblings are more likely to be sampled in the population, leading to a bias in the observed sibling distribution compared to the underlying fertility distribution.
For example, if we have a population of \(N_0\) women where \(50\%\) of women have 0 children, \(25\%\) of women have 1 child, and \(25\%\) of women have 2 children, the fertility distribution is \(P(N_{child}=0)=0.5\), \(P(N_{child}=1)=0.25\), and \(P(N_{child}=2)=0.25\). Thus, in the next generation, the population size is \[N_1 = 0.5N_0 \cdot 0 + 0.25N_0 \cdot 1 + 0.25N_0 \cdot 2 = 0.75N_0.\] And \(0.25N_0 \cdot 1\) individuals have 0 siblings, \(0.25N_0 \cdot 2\) individuals have 1 sibling. Therefore, the sibling distribution is \[P(N_{sib}=0)=\frac{0.25N_0}{0.75N_0}=\frac{1}{3},\quad P(N_{sib}=1)=\frac{0.25N_0 \cdot 2}{0.75N_0}=\frac{2}{3}.\]
To conclude, most people come from large families even when most families are small. This apparent paradox occurs because individuals with more siblings are sampled with an importance weight proportional to their family size.
Formally, the relationship between the fertility distribution and sibling distribution can be expressed as follows: Let \(N_{child}\) be the random variable representing the number of children per woman, and \(N_{sib}\) be the random variable representing the number of siblings per person in the next generation. Then, for any count \(k \geq 0\), the probability that an individual has \(k\) siblings is proportional to the probability that their mother has \(k+1\) children, multiplied by the number of ways the individual could be one of those children. Therefore, we have: \[P(N_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(N_{child}=k+1). \tag{3}\] This relationship holds for any fertility distribution, and the sibling distribution can be derived accordingly.

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
section: 2.2 Sibling distribution for different fertility distributions

title: Family size findability
text:
We aim to clarify the probability of finding at least one cousin in a database, given the family structure in the PODFRIDGE project. This probability depends on two key factors: the number of relatives—determined by the family size (number of children per family) distribution—and the database coverage, which is the proportion of individuals in the database relative to the total population.
Erlich’s earlier paper modeled the family size distribution using a Poisson distribution, a common approach in many genetic studies. However, this assumption doesn’t accurately capture real-world family sizes, which often show overdispersion and an excess of zeros (i.e., families with no children).
In this report, we simulate the family size distribution using a Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) distribution, which better reflects real-world data than the Poisson model. We then calculate the probability of finding at least one cousin in the database based on the simulated family sizes and different levels of database coverage.

title: Family size findability
text:
We aim to clarify the probability of finding at least one cousin in a database, given the family structure in the PODFRIDGE project. This probability depends on two key factors: the number of relatives—determined by the family size (number of children per family) distribution—and the database coverage, which is the proportion of individuals in the database relative to the total population.
Erlich’s earlier paper modeled the family size distribution using a Poisson distribution, a common approach in many genetic studies. However, this assumption doesn’t accurately capture real-world family sizes, which often show overdispersion and an excess of zeros (i.e., families with no children).
In this report, we simulate the family size distribution using a Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) distribution, which better reflects real-world data than the Poisson model. We then calculate the probability of finding at least one cousin in the database based on the simulated family sizes and different levels of database coverage.

title: Family size findability
text:
2 Methods

2.1 Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) Distribution
Based on the empirical data from the US, we find that the distribution of family size is better modeled as a Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) distribution. The ZINB distribution is a more realistic family size distribution, as it accounts for the excess zeros (families with no children) and the overdispersion often observed in family size data.
The Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial (ZINB) distribution is a statistical model that combines a negative binomial distribution with an additional process that generates excess zeros. It is characterized by three parameters: the zero-inflated probability \(p\), the mean number \(\mu\) and the size \(s\) of the negative binomial distribution, denoted by \(\text{ZINB}(p, s, \mu) = p\delta_0 + (1-p)\text{NB}(s,\mu)\). The average family size is \((1-p)\mu\). Based on empirical data, we set the parameters to \(p=0.1, ~ s=3, ~ \mu = 3\).


2.2 Calculating the Number of Relatives
In this report, we only consider the relatives whose generation difference between the individual and the relative is less than or equal to 1. Therefore, the first-degree relatives include parents, siblings, and children; the second-degree relatives include aunts, uncles, nephews, nieces, and half-siblings; and the third-degree relatives include first cousins. The number of relatives is calculated based on the family size distribution simulated by the ZINB distribution. For now, the number of half-siblings is set to 0.
Specifically, if one couple is known as the ancestor couple of the individual, this couple has at least one child, and the number of children is simulated from the ZINB distribution conditional on at least one child. For simplicity, we assume that \(X\) follows a ZINB distribution and \(Y\) follows a ZINB distribution conditional on at least one child minus one.
The number of first-degree relatives is given by \(2+Y+X\). The number of second-degree relatives is given by \(4 + \sum_{i=1}^2Y_i + \sum_{i=1}^{Y}X_i\). The number of third-degree relatives is given by \(\sum_{i=1}^{\sum_{j=1}^2 Y_j} X_i\).


2.3 The Probability of Finding at Least One Cousin in the Database
The number of cousins is simulated from the ZINB distribution as above. The simulated data is denoted as \(\{r_k\}_{k=1}^n\), where \(n\) is the number of simulations. For each simulated number of cousins \(r_k\), we assume that the number of cousins found in the database follows a binomial distribution with parameters \(r_k\), the genetic match probability \(p\), and the coverage of the database \(c\), denoted as \(\text{Binomial}(r_k, cp)\). The probability of finding at least one cousin in the database is therefore \(1-(1-cp)^{r_k}\). The expected probability across all simulations is given by \(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^n (1-(1-cp)^{r_k})\).

title: About
text: This project investigates long-range familial search capabilities in genetic databases. Using statistical models of fertility and family structure, we estimate how likely it is to identify individuals through their distant relatives (cousins) who may be present in genetic databases.

title: About
text: This project investigates long-range familial search capabilities in genetic databases. Using statistical models of fertility and family structure, we estimate how likely it is to identify individuals through their distant relatives (cousins) who may be present in genetic databases.

title: About
text:
2 Methods

2.1 Fertility Modeling
We use a zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) distribution to model fertility patterns:
\[P(X = k) = \begin{cases}
\pi + (1 - \pi) \cdot NB(0; \mu, \theta) & \text{if } k = 0 \\
(1 - \pi) \cdot NB(k; \mu, \theta) & \text{if } k > 0
\end{cases}\]
where: - \(\pi\) is the zero-inflation probability (excess childlessness) - \(\mu\) is the mean of the negative binomial component - \(\theta\) is the dispersion parameter


2.2 Sibling Distribution
The sibling distribution is derived from the fertility distribution, using the relationship: \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(num_{child}=k+1).\]


2.3 Genetic Match Probability
The probability of a genetic match is modelled based on a binomial distribution \(\text{Binomial}(r, cp)\), where \(r\) is the number of cousins, \(c\) is the database coverage, and \(p\) is the genetic match probability.

title: About
text:
3 Software
This project uses:

R for statistical computing
Shiny for interactive visualizations
workflowr for reproducible research
pscl for zero-inflated models
ggplot2 for data visualization

title: About
text:
4 Authors
This research is conducted at the Lasisi Lab.
